anomalies.description: Checkout failures coincided with payment-provider TLS handshake rejections, while the payment-service TLS renewal job that normally logs confirmation has no 23:05 scheduler entry.
The deploy was rolled back at 23:25, but the error rate remained unchanged until 23:40, contrary to the declared behavior of a deploy-caused failure.
The database-overload hypothesis conflicts with database latency remaining 22-35ms, below the declared 50ms normal limit, during the incident.

evidence.content: Deploy completed 23:00. Rollback completed 23:25. Error rate unchanged by rollback; errors ceased 23:40.
evidence.label: Deploy and rollback log

evidence.content: Database latency stable at 22-35ms throughout the incident window.
evidence.label: Database latency metrics

evidence.content: Payment API rejecting connections 23:10-23:40 with TLS handshake failures. Connections resume 23:40.
evidence.label: Payment provider log

evidence.content: Entries present for 22:00 and 00:00 jobs. No entry for the 23:05 TLS renewal job tonight. Retry sweep runs at 23:38.
evidence.label: Cron scheduler log

case.expected_behavior: Deploys either cause immediate errors or none; a rollback removes any deploy-caused failure within minutes. The nightly TLS certificate renewal cron for the payment service runs at 23:05 and always writes a log line confirming renewal. Payment API calls succeed with valid certificates. Database latency stays under 50ms at that traffic level.
case.observed_outcome: E-commerce checkout returned a spike of HTTP 500 errors from 23:10 to 23:40. The on-call team blamed the 23:00 deploy and rolled it back at 23:25, but errors continued until 23:40 and then stopped on their own.

coherence.explanation: H3 explains the missing 23:05 renewal confirmation, the 23:10-23:40 TLS rejections, and why rollback did not change the error rate; H1 does not explain rollback ineffectiveness, and H2 conflicts with stable latency. The score is below complete coherence because no evidence states the certificate's validity, expiry, or serial at the failure time, and E4 records a retry sweep but not its result.

expected_absent.description: The 23:05 TLS renewal job's confirmation log line, which the declared normal behavior says is always written, is absent from the scheduler log.
expected_absent.significance: This is an observable absence anchored to the stated renewal behavior and directly distinguishes a local renewal failure from an explanation confined to the deploy or database.

expected_absent.description: Error recovery within minutes of the 23:25 rollback is absent; the error rate remained unchanged until 23:40.
expected_absent.significance: A deploy-caused failure should have cleared shortly after rollback under the declared normal behavior. Its absence materially weakens the deploy hypothesis.

expected_present.description: Database latency remained below the declared 50ms normal threshold, at 22-35ms throughout the incident window.
expected_present.significance: This normal observation provides a direct baseline against the claim that database overload caused the checkout errors.

unexpected_absent.description: The elevated database-latency episode that database overload should have produced is absent from the incident metrics.
unexpected_absent.significance: The absence of latency above 50ms is a failed prediction of the database-overload hypothesis.

unexpected_present.description: Checkout returned a spike of HTTP 500 errors from 23:10 to 23:40.
unexpected_present.significance: This is the outcome requiring explanation; E3 ties the same window to payment TLS handshake failures.

unexpected_present.description: The payment provider rejected connections with TLS handshake failures from 23:10 to 23:40.
unexpected_present.significance: This is the strongest direct technical symptom associated with the checkout-error window and points away from a database-only explanation.

hypotheses.statement: The 23:05 payment-service TLS renewal did not execute, leaving the service unable to complete payment-provider TLS handshakes until the 23:38 retry sweep restored a usable certificate state.

supported_by.reason: E4 supports H3 because the normally logged 23:05 renewal entry is absent while adjacent scheduler entries are present, and it records a retry sweep at 23:38.
E3 supports H3 because TLS handshake failures cover 23:10-23:40 and connections resume shortly after the recorded retry sweep.
E1 supports H3 because rollback did not alter the error rate, which is consistent with a failure outside the deployed checkout code.

hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: A payment-service certificate audit showing that the certificate used from 23:10 to 23:40 was valid and unchanged by the 23:38 retry sweep, together with evidence that the 23:05 renewal completed successfully despite the missing scheduler entry.

contradicted_by.reason: E4 weakens H4 because a provider-side TLS incident does not explain why the payment service's normally logged local renewal job has no 23:05 entry.

hypotheses.statement: An independent payment-provider TLS-side incident caused the handshake rejections; the missing 23:05 renewal log is unrelated.

supported_by.reason: E3 supports H4 because it directly records payment-provider connection rejection with TLS handshake failures during the checkout-error window.

hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: Provider incident records or connection diagnostics showing no provider-side TLS fault while the payment service's certificate state changed from unusable to usable after the 23:38 retry sweep.

contradicted_by.reason: E1 weakens H1 because rollback completed at 23:25 but the error rate remained unchanged until 23:40; a deploy-caused failure should have cleared within minutes under the declared behavior.
E3 weakens H1 because the observed failure mechanism is payment TLS handshake rejection, not evidence that deployed checkout code failed.

hypotheses.statement: The 23:00 deploy broke the checkout flow.

supported_by.reason: E1 makes H1 a candidate because the deploy preceded the error window, but timing alone does not establish causation and rollback behavior contradicts the hypothesis.

hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: A comparison showing that the rolled-back and deployed checkout versions made the same payment request and both encountered the same TLS handshake rejection from 23:10 to 23:40.

contradicted_by.reason: E2 kills H2 as stated: database latency remained 22-35ms throughout the window, below the declared under-50ms normal behavior at this traffic level, rather than showing the elevated latency predicted by overload.
E3 further weakens H2 because it records TLS handshake failures at the payment provider during the precise error window.

hypotheses.resurrection_condition: Revive only if corrected database telemetry for 23:10-23:40 shows latency above 50ms or a database saturation event causally producing the checkout 500 responses.
hypotheses.statement: Database overload under evening traffic caused the checkout errors.
hypotheses.would_be_refuted_by: Database telemetry covering the affected checkout requests that shows latency remained below 50ms and no saturation throughout 23:10-23:40.

learning.summary: Baseline confidences are set from the direct observations. H3 leads because the expected 23:05 renewal confirmation is absent, payment TLS failures occur during the error window, and the retry sweep precedes recovery. H4 remains plausible because E3 identifies TLS rejection but does not establish whether the fault was local or provider-side; E4 weakens it by introducing an unexplained local renewal absence. H1 is seriously weakened because rollback did not change errors, so deploy timing alone is insufficient. H2 is rejected because its predicted latency elevation is absent in E2.

updates.reason: Baseline: E4's missing expected renewal entry and retry sweep align with E3's TLS-failure window and recovery; E1 excludes rollback response expected for a deploy cause.
Baseline: E3 directly supports a TLS-side failure, but E4's missing local renewal entry is unexplained by an independent provider incident.
Baseline: E1 supplies temporal proximity to deployment but also shows unchanged errors after rollback, the missing effect H1 should have produced.
Baseline: E2 shows 22-35ms latency throughout, directly contradicting the overload prediction.

mirror_question: If H3 is false, the clue likely being overweighted is the timing of the 23:38 retry sweep relative to recovery; E4 does not state that the sweep successfully renewed or replaced a certificate.

missing_evidence.description: The payment-service certificate audit for 23:05-23:40: certificate serial, validity or expiry state, and whether the 23:38 retry sweep changed it. This is needed to test the renewal-failure interpretation suggested by the missing normal confirmation log.
missing_evidence.impact_if_found: An expired, invalid, or replaced certificate after the retry sweep would strongly raise H3 and weaken H4. A valid unchanged certificate across the interval would weaken H3 and shift weight toward H4 or another TLS path.

missing_evidence.description: The expected post-rollback recovery event is absent: no error-rate reduction occurred within minutes after the 23:25 rollback, as E1 records.
missing_evidence.impact_if_found: If corrected rollback or error-rate records show prompt recovery, H1 would revive substantially. If they confirm unchanged errors through 23:40, H1 remains weakened.

missing_evidence.description: An elevated database-latency episode that the database-overload hypothesis predicts is absent from the supplied incident metrics; E2 instead records 22-35ms latency.
missing_evidence.impact_if_found: Corrected metrics showing latency above 50ms or saturation would reopen H2. Confirmation that latency stayed below 50ms leaves H2 rejected.

next_test.description: Retrieve the payment service's certificate and renewal audit covering 23:05-23:40, including the certificate serial and validity before and after the 23:38 retry sweep.

outcome_map.observed_result: The certificate was expired, invalid, or absent before 23:38, and the retry sweep installed or renewed a usable certificate before connections resumed.
The certificate remained valid and unchanged across 23:05-23:40, with no successful local certificate change attributable to the retry sweep.
No certificate audit is retained for the interval.

open_case_index.explanation: A1 remains open because the case lacks certificate validity and renewal-result evidence: E4 proves the normal scheduler confirmation is absent but not that certificate state caused E3's TLS failures. H4 also remains viable because E3 does not identify whether the TLS fault originated locally or at the provider. A2 and A3 are substantially explained by non-deploy TLS failure and stable database latency.

prime_suspect.absolving_datum: A certificate audit showing a valid unchanged payment-service certificate throughout 23:05-23:40 and no successful change caused by the 23:38 retry sweep.
prime_suspect.condemning_datum: A certificate audit showing the 23:05 renewal did not occur, the certificate was unusable during the TLS failures, and the 23:38 retry sweep restored a usable certificate before recovery.
prime_suspect.justification: H3 explains A1's missing renewal confirmation and TLS failures, A2's unchanged error rate after rollback, and is compatible with A3's normal database latency. H4 explains the TLS failures but leaves the missing local renewal log unexplained. H3 still faces the unobserved certificate state and the fact that E4 does not report the retry sweep's result.
